openings.name: Queen's Gambit
openings.overview: The Queen's Gambit is one of the oldest and most respected openings for White. By offering the c-pawn on move two, White challenges Black's central pawn on d5 and fights for space and development. Most strong players decline the gambit, leading to rich positional battles rather than wild tactics.
goals: Establish a strong pawn center with d4 and e3/e4
Pressure the c6 and e6 squares and the d5 pawn
Prepare a minority attack on the queenside in many lines
advantages: Solid, principled play with clear strategic ideas
White often gets a space advantage and easier development
Black must play accurately to equalize; small errors can be punished
Leads to middlegames where White's plans are well understood
disadvantages: The gambit is not truly 'won' — Black can hold comfortably with correct play
Some lines (Slav, Semi-Slav) are very drawish at high levels
Requires patience; fewer early tactical shots than 1.e4 openings
Black's ...c6 or ...e6 structures can be very solid
longTermPlans: Break with e4 when the center is prepared
Queenside minority attack (...b5-b4 ideas for White in QGD)
Trade dark-squared bishops and target weak dark squares
Use the c-file after exchanges on c5 or cxd5

mainline.name: Orthodox Queen's Gambit Declined
mainline.notes: White builds a strong center with e3 and develops naturally. Black solidifies with ...Be7 and ...Nbd7, keeping the d5 pawn defended. The position is balanced but rich in strategic ideas.

sidelines.name: Queen's Gambit Accepted
sidelines.notes: Black accepts the pawn but must return it later or face a strong White center. White often recaptures with e3 and Bxc4, gaining rapid development and central control.

sidelines.name: Slav Defense
sidelines.notes: Black supports d5 without blocking the c8-bishop. Very solid and popular at all levels. White may continue with Nc3, Nf3, or the Exchange Variation.

sidelines.name: Semi-Slav
sidelines.notes: Combines Slav structure with QGD flexibility. Black gets a very solid position but the bishop on c8 can be passive. A main battleground in modern chess.

openings.name: Vienna Gambit
openings.tagline: Sacrifice a pawn for rapid development
openings.overview: The Vienna Gambit is an aggressive way to fight for the initiative against 1...e5. After 2...Nf6 (the Vienna Game), White strikes with 3.f4, offering a pawn to open lines and accelerate development. It suits players who prefer dynamic, attacking positions over slow maneuvering.
goals: Open the f-file and gain kingside attacking chances
Develop quickly with Nc3, d4, and Bc4 or Be2
Seize space with e5 when Black takes on f4
Create tactical threats against the black king
advantages: Surprises opponents who expect quiet Vienna Game lines
Leads to open, tactical positions where White's lead in development matters
Black must defend accurately from early on
Less theory-heavy than mainline Ruy Lopez or Italian
disadvantages: Objectively the pawn sacrifice may not be enough for a full advantage
Black can decline with 3...d5 or 3...Nc6 for solid equality
If White overextends, the missing f-pawn becomes a weakness
Requires good tactical vision to justify the gambit
longTermPlans: Push d4 and e5 to cramp Black's position
Castle kingside and bring rooks to the f-file
Target f7 and the weakened kingside dark squares
Transition to a favorable endgame if Black returns the material

mainline.name: Main Gambit Line
mainline.notes: Black takes the pawn but the queen move to e7 blocks the f8-bishop. White builds a powerful pawn center with d4 and e5, gaining space and open lines for a kingside attack.

sidelines.name: Declined with 3...d5
sidelines.notes: Black strikes back in the center instead of taking the pawn. White can capture on d5 or play exd5; the position becomes sharp but less gambit-like.

sidelines.name: Vienna Game (3...Nc6)
sidelines.notes: Black develops the knight instead of playing ...Nf6. White can still push f4 for a King's Gambit-style attack, or play Bc4 for quieter development.

sidelines.name: Quiet Vienna (3.g3)
sidelines.notes: A positional alternative to the gambit. White fianchettos the bishop and delays f4, aiming for a flexible setup without immediate pawn sacrifices.

lichessResources.name: Opening Explorer from 3.f4
lichessResources.description: Interactive tree from the gambit pawn push

openings.name: Caro-Kann
openings.overview: The Caro-Kann is one of the most reliable defenses to 1.e4. Black prepares ...d5 without weakening the pawn structure, building a sturdy wall that is hard for White to break. The Classical Variation with ...dxe4 and ...Bf5 gives Black active piece play while maintaining solidity.
goals: Develop the light-squared bishop outside the pawn chain (...Bf5 or ...Bg4)
Keep a solid pawn structure with c6 and e6
Neutralize White's space advantage with careful piece placement
advantages: Very solid — Black rarely gets a bad position out of the opening
Clear development plan for the c8-bishop (unlike the French Defense)
Leads to positions where Black understands the middlegame plans
Less tactical trickery required than in Sicilian lines
disadvantages: Black's light-squared bishop can become a problem if trapped or exchanged
White often gets a space advantage and easier queenside play
Some lines (Advance, Exchange) can be slightly passive for Black
Requires knowledge of several sub-variations to handle all White setups
longTermPlans: Break with ...c5 or ...e5 when the center is ready
Use the c-file after ...cxd4 and recapture
Trade pieces to reduce White's space advantage
Target isolated or backward pawns in the endgame

mainline.name: Classical Variation
mainline.notes: Black exchanges on d4 and develops the bishop to f5 before playing ...e6. This is the most principled way to activate the c8-bishop and fight for equality.

sidelines.name: Advance Variation
sidelines.notes: White gains space and locks the structure. Black responds with ...Bf5 or ...c5 to undermine the center. Sharp and positional — Black must know the pawn breaks.

sidelines.name: Exchange Variation
sidelines.notes: White simplifies to an open position with an isolated d-pawn for Black. Black gets easy development and can pressure the d4 pawn.

sidelines.name: Panov-Botvinnik Attack
sidelines.notes: White strikes in the center with c4, leading to IQP (isolated queen pawn) positions. Black develops normally and targets the d4 pawn.

lichessResources.name: Caro-Kann Classical
lichessResources.description: Full classical line with ...Bf5 and ...e6 setups

lichessResources.name: Caro-Kann Overview
lichessResources.description: All major Caro-Kann branches on Lichess

openings.name: King's Indian
openings.overview: The King's Indian Defense lets White build a big center, then Black strikes back with ...e5 or ...c5. The fianchettoed bishop on g7 becomes a powerful piece, eyeing the long diagonal. It is a favorite of fighting players who enjoy dynamic, unbalanced positions.
goals: Allow White's center, then challenge it with ...e5 or ...c5
Fianchetto the kingside bishop to g7 for long-diagonal pressure
Castle kingside and prepare ...f5 or ...Nfd7-Nf6 rerouting
Create kingside counterplay against White's queenside expansion
advantages: Rich, dynamic positions with winning chances for Black
White's overextended center can become a target
The g7-bishop is often Black's best piece in the middlegame
Teaches important strategic themes: pawn storms, piece maneuvers, opposite-side castling
disadvantages: White can get a large space advantage if Black is too slow
Some lines (Samisch, Four Pawns) are very dangerous for Black
Requires precise timing for the ...e5 or ...c5 breaks
Can be cramped in the early middlegame before counterplay arrives
longTermPlans: Play ...f5-f4 to open the kingside, or ...Nf6-g4-e5 maneuvers
Trade the g7-bishop for White's c1-bishop when advantageous
Use the e5 square for knights after ...e5 exchange
Launch a kingside pawn storm if White castles queenside

mainline.name: Classical King's Indian
mainline.notes: White builds the classical big center. Black castles and plays ...e5, challenging d4. Both sides have clear plans: White may expand on the queenside; Black seeks kingside counterplay.

sidelines.name: Samisch Variation
sidelines.notes: White plays f3 to support e4 and prepare a kingside castle. Black often responds with ...c5 or ...b5. Sharp and theoretical — Black must know the pawn breaks.

sidelines.name: Four Pawns Attack
sidelines.notes: White grabs maximum space with f4. Black must counter quickly with ...c5 or ...e5 before being overrun. Very tactical and double-edged.

sidelines.name: Fianchetto Variation (White)
sidelines.notes: White also fianchettos, leading to a quieter positional battle. Black can play ...Nbd7, ...e5, or ...c5 depending on taste.

lichessResources.name: Classical King's Indian
lichessResources.description: Deep classical lines with ...e5 and queenside plans
